title: Other Computer Related Services

narrative.executive_view: This residual code is heterogeneous and its revenue mix is unmeasured. The best available MSP proxy shows growth in security, cloud management and infrastructure support, while platform tools automate portions of provisioning and recovery. The investable question is therefore not whether managed IT demand vanishes, but whether a target owns recurring accountable outcomes rather than commodity project hours.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI and automation affect triage, documentation, configuration scripts, monitoring and routine support. Microsoft documents zero-touch device deployment without traditional imaging, and Azure documents managed replication, failover and failback; these are bounded examples of vendor-supplied workflow automation, not evidence that all installation or recovery services disappear. Field deployment, recovery design, security judgment and client escalation remain human work.
narrative.value_capture: A recurring managed-service or DR contract can retain some efficiency, whereas project, incident and resale-plus-services books face competitive pass-through. Vendor platforms package more routine work upstream, so a small provider needs the client relationship and accountability for an outcome to retain value. The available sources do not measure capture for 541519, so this remains target-level diligence rather than a population claim.
narrative.adoption_timing: MSP benchmark evidence shows AI is helping with repetitive tasks, but it does not establish that most residual-code firms have effective delivery-wide adoption. The selected rate remains below 50%, so timing is positive rather than mechanically zero. The proxy and vendor framing make this a low-confidence estimate.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The 705 estimated targets include businesses that MSP buyers may exclude for low MRR or high resale/project mix. M&A benchmarks reward retention and recurring revenue, so selected multiples are below a high-quality MSP case and assume only limited quality improvement. The selected consolidator count is an unsupported sensitivity assumption, not a buyer tally.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE, with important economic pressure. The MSP proxy reports structurally growing managed-IT demand from cybersecurity, cloud adoption and regulation; the Kaseya benchmark also reports revenue growth in security, cloud management and infrastructure support. Zero-touch provisioning and vendor-native DR capabilities can commoditize bounded routine delivery tasks, but these sources do not establish that the residual code's service base is shrinking or that vendors absorb accountable recovery, security and client-governance work. Treat durable demand as a recurring-accountability thesis, not a claim that project hours or margins are durable.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: MSP evidence is a sibling-code proxy, while 541519 is a catch-all. No code-specific owner-age source or revenue split was found, and vendor documentation demonstrates capabilities rather than displacement rates. Revenue mix, MRR, resale pass-through, and the actual share of DR versus project work remain decisive diligence items; a project/resale-heavy target can still have materially weaker economics than the durable-demand proxy.

sources.title: Kaseya 2025 MSP benchmark growth trends
sources.what_it_supports: MSP survey evidence that AI removes repetitive tasks and that revenue is growing in security, cloud management, and infrastructure support.

sources.title: ConnectWise PSA powered by Asio and Pro launch
sources.what_it_supports: Vendor packaging of AI automation across MSP PSA, RMM, security, and data-protection operations.

sources.title: Mayfaire Row B2B professional services acquisition guide
sources.what_it_supports: MSP multiple ranges, the premium for retention and recurring service revenue, and the guide's statement that managed-IT demand is structurally growing because of cybersecurity, cloud adoption, and regulation.

sources.title: Deploy Windows devices using Windows Autopilot
sources.what_it_supports: A bounded vendor documentation example of device deployment without traditional imaging, including profile assignment, monitoring, and troubleshooting.

sources.title: About Azure Site Recovery
sources.what_it_supports: A bounded vendor-native DR example: Azure Site Recovery manages replication, failover, and failback from a portal, while supporting both Azure and on-premises workloads.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: MSP tooling and bounded vendor-native provisioning and DR examples support automation of repetitive technical and administrative tasks, but physical deployment, recovery accountability, and escalation remain.
caveats: MSP evidence is a sibling-code proxy.
Role mix is four-digit 5415, not this residual code.
S4 and S5 demonstrate vendor capabilities, not a 541519 displacement rate.

role_judgments.rationale: Scripts and configuration automate; deployment judgment persists.
Mixed technical work includes deployable automation.
Status and scheduling automate; escalation persists.
Documentation and process work is assisted.
Tail mixes physical technicians with administration.

pi_dist.rationale: Only recurring contracted delivery can plausibly retain much efficiency; project and resale books are more exposed to pass-through, but no 541519 pricing mix is measured.
caveats: The cited sources do not measure AI-savings pass-through or retention.

pi_moat.rationale: Integrated operational tooling and account knowledge create modest switching costs, while vendors package more automation upstream.
caveats: Platform evidence is not a small-firm moat measure.

t50_years.rationale: Observed task benefits support progress, but current effective adoption is below 50% for this heterogeneous proxy population.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Kaseya reports meaningful task benefit but not delivery-wide penetration; discounted effective use is below 50%.

historical_analogs.value: RMM, PSA, zero-touch provisioning, and managed replication have automated routine IT operations; AI extends automation into triage and documentation.
historical_analogs.rationale: Vendor documentation supports workflow automation capabilities, not a historical adoption rate for 541519.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Incremental automation to broader agent-assisted operations

selection.rationale: No code-specific owner-age source was found.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No documented 541519 succession shortage was found.
caveats: Absence is not proof of no firm-level need.

active_consolidators.rationale: MSP deal evidence supports an active but quality-screened buyer pool; six is an unsupported sensitivity assumption rather than a 541519 buyer tally.
caveats: S3 provides valuation ranges but no buyer count.

buy_mult.rationale: Below the 6x-7x well-run-MSP median because residual firms can be project/resale-heavy.
caveats: MSP benchmark is a scope proxy.

exit_mult.rationale: Assumes improved contracted recurring mix, within the cited high-quality MSP range.
caveats: Expansion requires evidence of MRR and retention.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed project, incident, resale-plus-services, and recurring managed/DR revenue; recurring contracts may retain more automation savings.
pricing_structure.rationale: Benchmark and M&A evidence distinguish recurring MSP quality from project work, but do not measure 541519's revenue split or AI-savings pass-through.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Project/resale dominated to recurring managed-service dominated
caveats: Target-level contract and revenue-mix diligence is required.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Task exposure and adoption are separate: role fractions measure automatable tasks; the below-50% adoption estimate produces positive t50.

terminal_value.justification: Recurring managed-IT demand is the available MSP proxy's growth direction, driven by cybersecurity, cloud adoption, and regulation. S4 and S5 show bounded vendor automation of provisioning and DR tasks, so they support delivery and margin pressure but do not establish shrinkage of the residual service base or replacement of accountable recovery and governance.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: MSP sources are explicitly treated as sibling-code proxies, and S4/S5 are bounded vendor-capability examples rather than 541519 prevalence evidence.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Project and resale work may pass automation savings to clients; this is modeled in pi_dist, not counted again in task exposure.

dataset_mismatch.description: The role mix is four-digit 5415 and receipts predate current AI and DRaaS shifts.
sensitivity: A field-technician-heavy six-digit mix would lower task exposure.
Post-shift revenue mix could change labor share.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: No sourced dominant 541519 subsegment established; recurring MSP-like accountable operations are used only as a disclosed sibling-code proxy for terminal demand.
market_boundary.selection_basis: Residual NAICS classification and source proxies do not establish revenue shares. S4 and S5 bound vendor capabilities to device deployment and managed DR rather than proving displacement across the code.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Timing changes materially if effective adoption crosses 50%.
Capture varies with recurring versus project revenue.
Exit depends on proven MRR improvement.

reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates the terminal contradiction: vendor-native provisioning and DR evidence is now limited to the documented workflows, while the terminal class follows the cited recurring-MSP demand-growth proxy. Verify a target's recurring-accountability revenue rather than inferring it from this residual code.
